speaker: interviewer
text: Charlotte Chaney, May 23, 1995. Mrs. Cheney, we were talking about the condition of the inmates. And you said the greatest problems were their diseases.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the most difficult job you had in treating them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was malnutrition. We had to be careful what we gave them to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have to be fed intravenously, or could most of them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did-- we gave plasma to quite a few of the inmates, plus some IVs. And you had-- you just had to be careful what they ate at the very beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you handle the unsanitary conditions and make them sanitary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had, as I said, the barracks were cleaned out by the-- our own GIs, which we got supplies. And we would-- we had soap. And we had plenty of water. So when we washed them, and cleaned them, and then gave them clean gowns-- the Army did supply us with that-- and put them into a bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a palpable psychological change as these people were cleaned up and finally fed some decent food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. Definitely. I mean, you could see it. Some-- they-- a lot of them, a great deal of them, were ambulatory, where they could get out and walk around, which they did around the barracks, and try to help those that couldn't. You could see a little bit of hope in their eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the men, or the women, or the children fair any better than the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When we got there, they was just as bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in terms of their convalescence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. No. We-- we took-- we had to deal with them the same as any of the-- anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were-- were the children, by virtue of being children, able to get well faster, or were they all in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --such dire straits--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that it was just difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: So many of them were so sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were many of them able to then do any exercise? You said some of them could walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes. Yes. Some of them did, were able to.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there for what period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there about three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what changes did you see in the inmates over those three months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, they were responding to our treatment-- so many of them. And they were free to walk around and to talk to people. We tried to converse with them. We did have, as I said, some of the inmates that could speak some English finding out stories that they told us what happened, finding about their families. We did get the Army coming in, and so many people coming in, because they did have some trials at Dachau. They had the SS troopers there. So it become a very-- it became a very crowded place with Army personnel coming in and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what must've been deathly silent when you entered became a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a very bustling, busy place, Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the-- were you aware of a level that suddenly there was conversation among people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who discovered they had a chance to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must've been quite emotional and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very. Yes, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and uplifting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In retrospect, what would you have done differently over those three months?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I would have done anything differently, looking back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wouldn't have?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think we did the best that we could under those circumstances and knowing what we did know. I don't think we would've done anything differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you there only three months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other hospitals came in. The Army came in. And we were destined to go to the CBI. That's the China/Burma. And we had received orders. And when these orders came in, I found out that as a married nurse, I could go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you opted, instead of going from the frying pan into the fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the fire. But it seemed that I was attached then to another unit. Now, this was-- oh, in October. But the war ended in Japan. So the unit that I was attached to did not get home as fast as my own unit if I had stayed with it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: --reach the States. I found out about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you subsequently speak with liberators of other camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not see any liberators from any other--

speaker: interviewer
text: Never?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, never did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I-- I was-- unless you want to ask me some other questions, I can talk about one of the survivors that I recently met.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We'll get to-- we'll get to that in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back first to the letters you were writing home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction, because you were then also writing during the time you were in Dachau. What was the reaction of the people to whom you were writing the letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did not send the letters out until after the war was over. We couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept them all. And then I-- I sent the letters to my husband maybe four, five at a time. And he kept them. And so I did not know you know, any reaction or anything until I actually did get home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think anyone who did not witnesses it can really ever comprehend the scope of what when on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so, especially when you hear denials that it never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left Dachau, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to another unit stationed in-- in France, and-- because they had gotten-- they had received orders to go back to the States. So I stayed with that unit for awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are the memories of all this as vivid today as they were right after the war? Is this something that ever fades in your memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's always in the back of my memory. I mean, you'll never forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's always there.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what you saw did you tell your family and friends when you returned home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Because when I did return home, I was discharged. And then I went to Texas, back to Camp Boyd, because my husband was still in the Army. And he got discharged about three months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't want to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I didn't say or talk about it at all. I-- I just didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have one daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell her any of this as she was growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- she really didn't know too much about it. I started to talk about it, oh, in the '80s, 1980 or so, when I came down here. And then I heard about the Holocaust Center at FIU. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Holocaust Documentation and Education Center?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. That's right. And I contacted them. And actually, it was around that time that I started talk about it and take out my letters and pictures that I had and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter was raised Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How observant where you during the time she was growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We always belonged to a Temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had a very strong Jewish Identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was bar mitzvahed. She was 13. Definitely, we went down all the holidays, always had Shabbos. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know you had been in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: The family knew about it, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever try to question you about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. I mean, the-- even my whole-- my husband's family, when he went into business down here, then his brother came down to stay here. And his sister came down, involved in the business. And I never did talk too much about it-- or, you know, busy raising the children. And working down here, I wasn't-- I went into the operating room in 1953 at Mount Sinai Hospital. And I worked there for a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your wartime experiences made you more observant then you would have been? Have you done anything more in terms of supporting Israel or supporting Jewish causes than you might have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, indeed. Well, I made a vow when I came home that I would always do everything in my power if I could help anybody. My motto has been let me do, rather than anybody do for me. And that's been my way of life all these years. In the 1970s, I went to work with cystic fibrosis. My husband had a salesman that got married and had a baby that was CF. And at the time, I was not working for a couple years. I took off. And I did not know too much about the disease. And she had gone down to Jackson Memorial Hospital where they had a small clinic there. And his wife said to me that-- that they had mothers manning the clinic. And that if I wanted to know anything about it, he said, Charlotte, why don't you go down and see this [? Dr. McKee ?]-- he was the pediatrician-- and you know, couple of hours maybe, to help out or whatever you can do. And that's what happened. I went down there. First of all, I had to learn about the disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were not concentrating specifically on Jewish organizations, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, I did belong to B'nai B'rith.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just in your capacity as a nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I belonged to B'nai B'rith. I worked with B'nai B'rith. My husband-- and then we both joined the Jewish War Veterans, which we are members now for many, many years. In fact, I have dual membership in the women's auxiliary as well as in the Jewish War Veterans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anything in particular for the state of Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, through the Jewish War Veterans, we do support a hospital in Israel every year. We send money. We have a barbecue. We send money over. And we have sent equipment that we pay for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever-- have you ever kept in touch with any of the people you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out about one survivor about two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago, I was-- I went to Washington DC at the time that they-- I guess it's about two years that they opened up. Or last year, they opened up the museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's two years already. Yeah. Time goes so quickly. And the Jewish War Veterans has their national building in Washington DC and museum. And they have an exhibit of the Jewish GIs that went into the various camps and liberated them. These are the Jewish liberators. And I am the only Jewish nurse that they have in this exhibit. And they opened it up. And they asked me to come up, which I did for the opening of the exhibit. And this was going to be sent to different cities in the country. Well, the first city to open up was down here Miami. They had South Miami, Beth David, which they had an opening night that I was there. And a couple of days later, I received a call from a Mr. Herman Newman. He lives at Three Islands in Hallandale. And he said that he had read about the article in the paper. And he wanted to tell me that he was 15 years old and was just taken off one of the box cars when we came in, and Dachau was liberated, and that he would like to meet me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told him that for the past few years I have been working at The Diplomat Mall. I have a blood pressure unit which I-- that I am part of with a couple other nurses, that we take blood pressures for the people there, and for the doctors, and that we monitor. And I told him to meet me in Hallendale.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's only about five minutes away. And he came over with his wife. And I'm going to say it was a very tearful--

speaker: interviewer
text: I can imagine.

speaker: interviewee
text: --reunion. And he wanted to meet my husband. And we went up-- he invited us up Friday night for dinner, Shabbos dinner, which we did go. And I think we talked and talked and talked for hours. He told me about his life. He said that if it wasn't for us coming in, or the camp being liberated, and the nurses coming in taking care of the people there, he said within another two weeks, everybody would have been gone at Dachau. He said he was in one of the box cars, one of the few people remembering a soldier coming over and lifting him out of the boxcar. And he said he remembered the kind hands of the nurses taking care of him. He said that when we left, he was sent to England. And he said, for two years, until he was about 18, they took care of him in a hospital in England to help him walk. He said for a while he was only on crutches, and to overcome what had happened to him. He-- he came from Czechoslovakia. He had a mother. His father had passed away a couple years before. But he had a mother, and he had a brother and sisters. And they were all either burned up or gassed to death. He does not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he was among the last to arrive at Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how he had survived before that, all through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was sent to a couple of other camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said he had been sent to different camps and was put into this box car to come to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever-- have you ever gone to any of the survivor reunions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the-- well, I do belong to the Holocaust Center in Miami at FIU.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There I have been to meetings.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you've never come across anyone else from Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have not ever gone back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever made friends with any German people since the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I-- I have a group of friends that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever met any Germans with whom you've discussed any of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I have not.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were at an event or someplace where someone told you people over there are German, would you be tempted to go meet them or would you just as soon not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it wouldn't make any difference. I mean, today, this generation, it's like anything else, even here in the United States. It may not have been their fault, or-- you know, you just don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever-- I keep going back to this fact that Americans were so ignorant. Have you ever had any discussions in all these years with friends or family about the fact that Americans were so ignorant? And why? And do you fault anyone in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: I fault our government. I fault our president, our great Roosevelt, who everybody thought he was like a god. And I feel he was the one that was the worst antisemite you could find with, I believe, our Secretary of State that turned down, that would not let the Jewish people come into the United States, when the shipload of people came. They sent them to Cuba. And Cuba did not let them in. And they went back to Europe to be burned up or gassed to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people on the Saint Louis?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. So I feel they were-- I-- I fault our government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you find that other people over the years have had the same impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. More and more of it's coming to life now. That's really coming to light what had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the video you've made and that you travel around talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called And it's a very vivid documentation of the Jewish soldiers going into the camps and showing what happened. There's a segment there of Eisenhower coming into one of the camps, and then sending back to the-- to Congress, and to the President to send some of the people over to see just what happened, to get documentation of what the Germans had done. See--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who put this together. Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was done by a Mr. Hansen. He came in from Colorado. And he was the one that made this documentation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are there many copies of this that people travel around showing, or do you have one of the few copies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a copy that I have, and that he had sent me of it. And I don't know how many are available. I have been to a couple of organizations that I spoke at. And I have the address. And I tell them if they wanted it, there was even a phone number to call and to talk to him or whatever. So I don't know if they did or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have two boys, two grandsons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that you have come forward to tell this story, are you more tempted to discuss with them what your experiences were than you were to discuss it with their mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They-- they know. In fact, I may be going into my younger grandson's class. The teachers, he had told the teacher that his grandmother was a liberator. And I have a film called

speaker: interviewer
text: From the Holocaust Museum?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Holocaust-- yeah. And it's in one of the-- I got it from one of the libraries here to show. Because the film that I have, is-- I feel it's-- it's not right to show to small children. So I have And I'm making a date with one of his teachers to go into his class and show it.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what age do you think children are old enough to be told any of these stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, today what's going on into the schools, I feel if they're in the fifth grade, fifth, sixth grades and up, they should be aware of what-- just what hate can do. And we are finding so much of this antisemitism and what's going on now down here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you speak to children's groups or school groups, do you find any animosity from the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't find. It's just that-- the ignorance. That's what you find, ignorance.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Thank you for your questioning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Cheney, this is your husband, Bernard Cheney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for joining us, Mr. Chaney. I have one question for you. I'm impressed by the extent to which your wife has said no one in the United States seemed to really understand what was going on in Europe. Do you agree with that? BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): Oh, definitely. She's spoken before so many organizations and schools, and half of them are flabbergasted. How do you account for the fact that so few Americans knew at the time what was going on in Europe? BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): Because you have a new generation coming up right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, honey.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I'm talking about the-- at the time when you were in the Army. BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): The time when I was in the Army, the old-timers all know it. But they don't talk about it. Very few of them talk about it. But did you feel that when you were in the Army here, while your wife was overseas, that you had any concept at all about what going on in Europe at the time? BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): When she wrote me letters, I did. Before that I had no idea. Other than that, you would not have known? BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): No way. Thank you. BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): After she wrote me letters, I knew everything. And what was the reaction? Did you tell people about it? BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): Oh, definitely. What was their reaction? Did they believe it? BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): They were shocked. Everybody was shocked. And-- but they believed it? BERNARD CHANEY (HUSBAND): Oh, definitely. OK. Thank you. OK. Who is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's of myself and my husband. That was our wedding picture, taken August 20, 1944. This was-- we were married in Newark, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is a picture of you. What are the circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I received my commission as a first lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that you had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I went into the Army, we went in as a second lieutenant. We were given officer's status. And I became a first lieutenant in 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the highest rank you achieved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. OK. This says Dachau, May 1945. What is this building?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a building where we-- we stayed. That's where the German officers stayed. And we took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the day you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived at Dachau into the courtyard. And that-- our trucks are there. And we coming into Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is a certificate of appreciation signed by Elie Wiesel. What were the circumstances in which you received that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were having a conference here on the Holocaust Memorial Council. And this was presented to me at Barry University--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Miami.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Miami. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: This certificate represents part of the participation in the exhibition to which you referred in our interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who organized that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was for the Jewish War Veterans there and National Museum in Washington DC.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were represented as one of the Jewish War Veterans then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and as a liberator.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this photograph and the medal.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in March, 1945, from the South Broward Holocaust Center. They were honoring the survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, 1995. And they were honoring the survivors. And-- rather, they were honoring the liberators of the Holocaust. And on my right is one of the survivors. And on my left is Herman Newman, the survivor I'm-- that was at Dachau when I came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the medal?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the medal is an appreciation of our being a liberator of the concentration camps.
